title: Class Files and Instances
summary: Create a class in its own file, import it, and make separate objects from it.

sketchBlocks.title: Put a class in its own workspace file
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A class often belongs in its own workspace file so the rest of your program can import and use it.

For example, a small reading tracker might keep its `Book` class in a model file and create objects in another file:

```python
from models.book import Book

first_book = Book("Deep Work", 320)
second_book = Book("Atomic Habits", 280)

print(first_book.title)
print(second_book.pages)
```

Line by line:

- `from models.book import Book` brings the `Book` class into the current code.
- `first_book = Book("Deep Work", 320)` creates one instance.
- `second_book = Book("Atomic Habits", 280)` creates a different instance.
- `print(first_book.title)` reads data from the first object.
- `print(second_book.pages)` reads data from the second object.

The important idea is that the class is the blueprint, and each object made from it stores its own data.

sketchBlocks.title: Use __init__ to give each instance its own state
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
The `__init__` method runs when you create an object. It is where you usually store starting values on `self`.

Example:

```python
class Book:
    def __init__(self, title, pages):
        self.title = title
        self.pages = pages
```

Step by step:

- `class Book:` starts the class definition.
- `def __init__(self, title, pages):` defines the constructor.
- `self` means the specific object being created.
- `self.title = title` stores the incoming title on that object.
- `self.pages = pages` stores the incoming page count on that object.

If you create two books, each one gets its own `title` and `pages` values. That is why instances can behave like separate things even though they come from the same class.

sketchBlocks.title: Import the class and call methods on different objects
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Methods let an object do something with its own state.

Here is a simple example:

```python
class Book:
    def __init__(self, title, pages):
        self.title = title
        self.pages = pages

    def summary(self):
        return f"{self.title} has {self.pages} pages"
```

And then another part of the program can use it:

```python
from models.book import Book

morning_read = Book("Deep Work", 320)
evening_read = Book("Atomic Habits", 280)

print(morning_read.summary())
print(evening_read.summary())
```

Line by line:

- The class defines a method named `summary`.
- `return f"{self.title} has {self.pages} pages"` builds text from that object's attributes.
- `morning_read.summary()` calls the method on the first object.
- `evening_read.summary()` calls the same method on the second object.

The same class method can produce different results because each instance has different stored values.

quizDraft.title: Create a class in a model file
quizDraft.prompt: A library app should keep its `Book` class separate from the code that uses it. Complete the workspace so `models/book.py` defines a `Book` class with an `__init__(self, title, pages)` constructor that stores `title` and `pages`. Then update the main code so it creates `Book("Deep Work", 320)` and prints the title on one line and the page count on the next line.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# main.py
from models.book import Book

# Create one Book object below.
# Print its title, then print its pages.
quizDraft.solutionCode:
# main.py
from models.book import Book

book = Book("Deep Work", 320)
print(book.title)
print(book.pages)

files.content:
from models.book import Book

# Create one Book object below.
# Print its title, then print its pages.


n = 0
# keep file non-empty

semanticChecks.message: Define a class named Book in the model file.
Book should be constructible with title and pages.
Store title and pages on each Book instance.
Create one Book object in the main code.
Print the title and page count on separate lines.

quizDraft.hint: Define the class in `models/book.py`, then import it where the object is created.

help.concept: This exercise checks that you can place a class in its own workspace file, import it, and create an instance with stored attributes.
help.hint_1: In the constructor, assign the incoming values to `self.title` and `self.pages`.
help.hint_2: After importing `Book`, create one object and print its two attributes in order.

quizDraft.title: Store constructor values on separate instances
quizDraft.prompt: A reading club wants two separate `Book` objects. In `models/book.py`, finish the `Book` constructor so each object stores its own `title` and `pages`. In the main code, create `Book("Dune", 412)` and `Book("Emma", 300)`, then print the first book's title and the second book's page count.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# main.py
from models.book import Book

first_book = Book("Dune", 412)
second_book = Book("Emma", 300)

# Print the first book's title.
# Then print the second book's page count.
quizDraft.solutionCode:
# main.py
from models.book import Book

first_book = Book("Dune", 412)
second_book = Book("Emma", 300)

print(first_book.title)
print(second_book.pages)

files.content:
from models.book import Book

first_book = Book("Dune", 412)
second_book = Book("Emma", 300)

# Print the first book's title.
# Then print the second book's page count.

semanticChecks.message: Define a class named Book.
Book should accept title and pages in its constructor.
Each Book instance should store title and pages.
Create two Book objects in the main code.
Print two lines from the two different objects.

quizDraft.hint: Both objects come from the same class, but each one should keep its own attribute values.

help.concept: This exercise checks that `__init__` gives each instance its own state instead of sharing one value across all objects.
help.hint_1: Inside `__init__`, assign both incoming parameters to attributes on `self`.
help.hint_2: Create two different objects, then read one attribute from the first and a different attribute from the second.

quizDraft.title: Add and call an instance method
quizDraft.prompt: A library dashboard needs a short description for each book. In `models/book.py`, add a `summary(self)` method to `Book` that returns text in this format: `Title: <title>, Pages: <pages>`. In the main code, create `Book("Beloved", 275)` and print the result of calling `summary()`.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# main.py
from models.book import Book

book = Book("Beloved", 275)

# Print the summary for this book.
quizDraft.solutionCode:
# main.py
from models.book import Book

book = Book("Beloved", 275)
print(book.summary())

files.content:
from models.book import Book

book = Book("Beloved", 275)

# Print the summary for this book.

semanticChecks.message: Define the Book class.
Keep title and pages stored on the instance.
summary() should return the formatted description.
Create one Book object in the main code.
Print the returned summary.

quizDraft.hint: The method should return a string built from `self.title` and `self.pages`.

help.concept: This exercise checks that a method can use an object's stored attributes and that imported class methods can be called from the main code.
help.hint_1: Keep the constructor storing `title` and `pages`, then add a second method named `summary`.
help.hint_2: Return one formatted string from `summary`, and print `book.summary()` after creating the object.

quizDraft.title: Class or instance?
quizDraft.prompt: Which statements are correct about classes and instances in this topic? Choose all that apply.
options: A class is a blueprint for creating objects.
An instance is a specific object created from a class.
Importing a class automatically creates all objects you need.
Two instances from one class must always have identical attribute values.
quizDraft.hint: One choice describes the blueprint, and another describes the created object.

help.concept: A class defines structure and behavior, while an instance is one object created from that class.
help.hint_1: Look for the option that describes a reusable definition and the option that describes a specific created object.
help.hint_2: Reject choices that say every object shares one set of attribute values automatically or that imports create objects by themselves.

quizDraft.title: Complete an attribute assignment
quizDraft.prompt: Fill in the missing value so the constructor stores the incoming title on the object.
quizDraft.hint: The right side should use the parameter, not the object name.

help.concept: Inside `__init__`, you usually copy incoming parameter values onto `self` attributes.
help.hint_1: The left side names the attribute on the object. The blank is the constructor parameter being stored.
help.hint_2: Use the parameter that already holds the incoming book the missing term, not `self` and not `print`.

quizDraft.title: Order the steps to use a class from another workspace file
quizDraft.prompt: Put these steps in the correct order for using a class stored in its own workspace file.
tokens: Define the class in a model file
Import the class into the main code
Create an instance with constructor values
Print an attribute or method result
correctOrder: Define the class in a model file
Import the class into the main code
Create an instance with constructor values
Print an attribute or method result
quizDraft.hint: You need the class definition before you can import it and create objects from it.

help.concept: A typical workflow is define the class, import it, create an instance, then use its data or methods.
help.hint_1: Start with the class existing in its model file, not with printing.
help.hint_2: After the class exists, bring it into the main code, create an object, and only then read from it.

quizDraft.title: Project step: create the Account class
quizDraft.prompt: Start an account tracker. Define an `Account` class in `models/account.py` with an `__init__(self, owner, balance)` constructor that stores `owner` and `balance`. In the main code, create `Account("Ava", 120)` and print the owner on one line and the balance on the next line.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# main.py
from models.account import Account

# Create Ava's account and print owner then balance.
quizDraft.solutionCode:
# main.py
from models.account import Account

account = Account("Ava", 120)
print(account.owner)
print(account.balance)

files.content:
from models.account import Account

# Create Ava's account and print owner then balance.

semanticChecks.message: Define a class named Account.
Account should accept owner and balance.
Store owner and balance on the Account instance.
Create one Account object in the main code.
Print the owner and balance on separate lines.

quizDraft.hint: This step mirrors the book examples, but now the class represents an account.

help.concept: This project step checks that you can move a class into its own model file and create an instance from the main code.
help.hint_1: Store both constructor values on `self` inside `Account.__init__`.
help.hint_2: After importing `Account`, create one object for Ava and print its two attributes in order.

quizDraft.title: Project step: add account methods
quizDraft.prompt: Build on the account tracker. Keep the `Account` class with `owner` and `balance`, then add a `deposit(self, amount)` method that increases the balance and a `summary(self)` method that returns text in this format: `<owner>: $<balance>`. In the main code, create `Account("Ava", 120)`, call `deposit(30)`, and print the summary.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# main.py
from models.account import Account

account = Account("Ava", 120)

# Deposit 30, then print the summary.
quizDraft.solutionCode:
# main.py
from models.account import Account

account = Account("Ava", 120)
account.deposit(30)
print(account.summary())

files.content:
from models.account import Account

account = Account("Ava", 120)

# Deposit 30, then print the summary.

semanticChecks.message: Keep the Account class defined.
Account should still store owner and balance.
summary() should report the current owner and balance.
summary() should exist before any deposit.
deposit(30) should update the balance to 150.
Create one Account object in the main code.
Print the summary after depositing.

quizDraft.hint: One method changes state, and the other reports the current state.

help.concept: This step checks that methods can both update object state and return text based on the updated attributes.
help.hint_1: `deposit` should add the incoming amount to `self.balance`.
help.hint_2: After calling `deposit(30)`, the summary should reflect the new balance, not the original one.

quizDraft.title: Project step: track two separate accounts
quizDraft.prompt: Continue the account tracker from the previous step. Keep the `Account` class with `owner`, `balance`, `deposit`, and `summary`. In the main code, create `Account("Ava", 120)` and `Account("Noah", 90)`. Call `deposit(30)` on Ava's account only. Then print Ava's summary and Noah's summary on separate lines so the output shows that each object keeps its own state.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# main.py
from models.account import Account

ava_account = Account("Ava", 120)
noah_account = Account("Noah", 90)

# Deposit 30 into Ava's account only.
# Print Ava's summary, then Noah's summary.
quizDraft.solutionCode:
# main.py
from models.account import Account

ava_account = Account("Ava", 120)
noah_account = Account("Noah", 90)

ava_account.deposit(30)
print(ava_account.summary())
print(noah_account.summary())

files.content:
from models.account import Account

ava_account = Account("Ava", 120)
noah_account = Account("Noah", 90)

# Deposit 30 into Ava's account only.
# Print Ava's summary, then Noah's summary.

semanticChecks.message: Keep the Account class defined.
Each Account should store owner and balance.
Ava's account should update to 150 after deposit(30).
Noah's summary should still show the original balance.
Create two Account objects in the main code.
Print both account summaries on separate lines.

quizDraft.hint: Only one account should change, so the two summaries should end with different balances.

help.concept: This step checks that separate instances from the same class can change independently.
help.hint_1: Create two objects from the same class, but call `deposit` on only one of them.
help.hint_2: If both summaries show the same updated balance, your objects are not keeping separate state correctly.

quizDraft.title: Why keep the class in its own workspace file?
quizDraft.prompt: Why is it useful to keep a class like `Account` or `Book` in its own workspace file and import it into the main code? Choose all that apply.
options: It keeps the model definition organized in one place.
It makes the class reusable from other parts of the project.
It forces every instance to share the same attribute values.
It removes the need to create objects with the constructor.
quizDraft.hint: Think about reuse and keeping responsibilities organized.

help.concept: Separating a class into its own workspace file makes the code easier to reuse and keeps the model logic organized.
help.hint_1: Look for answers about organization and reuse, not answers about automatic object creation.
help.hint_2: A separate class file helps you import the same model in different parts of a project and keeps object behavior grouped together.

quizDraft.title: Complete the import statement
quizDraft.prompt: Fill in the missing class name so the main code can import the model correctly.
quizDraft.hint: The imported name should match the class defined in the model.

help.concept: An import statement must name the class you want to bring into the current code.
help.hint_1: The module path already points to `models.the missing term`. The blank is the class name.
help.hint_2: Use the capitalized class name from the topic examples, not the lowercase module name.

quizDraft.title: What does self refer to?
quizDraft.prompt: Inside a class method like `__init__` or `summary`, what does `self` refer to?
options: The specific instance using the method
The import statement for the class
Every instance in the whole program at once
A special keyword that only works in print statements
quizDraft.hint: It is not the class itself and not every object at once.

help.concept: In instance methods, `self` refers to the specific object the method is working with.
help.hint_1: Think about how `self.title` can be different for two objects from the same class.
help.hint_2: `self` points to the current instance whose method was called.
